stage1_token_cache_resident_estimate_mb.note: n_tokens(815632) x dim(128) x bit/8 quantized codes + ~1 f32 scale/token. This is the persistent code footprint a F-2 index would hold; the observed ~1.3 GiB steady backend RSS is PG machinery + per-call query-quant workspace + a ~28 MB/call leak (see harness notes), NOT these codes.

arch_notes: x86_64 AVX2 (turbovec SIMD kernels active; latency is meaningful). CPU torch only for embedding. pg16 shared_buffers=256MB, maintenance_work_mem=512MB.
memory_safety: All heavy embedding steps run under systemd-run --user --scope MemoryMax=12G MemorySwapMax=0. Sweep client reconnects every 40 queries to bound the colbert_search per-call backend-RSS leak (~28 MB/call) to a ~3.1 GiB peak; available RAM never dropped below ~17.7 GiB; postmaster never at risk; no kill -9.

verdict.best_recall_operating_point: bit_width=3-4, per_token_k=128, candidate_n=512 -> nDCG@10 0.619 (== exact ceiling 0.6184), Recall@10 0.7438
verdict.best_value_operating_point: bit_width=2-4, per_token_k=64-128, candidate_n=256 -> nDCG@10 ~0.618, Recall@10 ~0.742 at ~270-300ms; delta over baseline +0.062 nDCG / +0.090 Recall
verdict.quantization_erosion: Negligible. nDCG@10 across 2/3/4-bit at matched (ptk,cand) varies <=0.002 (e.g. cand=256/ptk=64: bw2=0.6179, bw3=0.6175, bw4=0.6175). The plan's single biggest risk (2-4 bit destroys token signal) DID NOT materialise on SciFact. 2-bit is sufficient.
verdict.delta_summary: colbert_search beats pooled+rerank at every config. Delta is largest where it matters: at small candidate budgets (cand=128: +0.103 nDCG / +0.135 Recall) the pooled baseline collapses (relevant docs fall outside the pooled top-N) while colbert_search retrieves them via best-single-token. Delta shrinks to +0.014 at cand=512 where the baseline eventually pulls the relevant docs in, but at ~2x the latency.
verdict.latency: Arm A is ~1.5-2.1x slower than Arm B at matched candidate_n (e.g. cand=256/bw4: A p50=315ms vs B p50=141ms; cand=128/bw4: A=150ms vs B=83ms). A's cost is dominated by the per-call backend-cache token-index BUILD (F-1 rebuilds it each call); a persistent F-2 index would amortise this away, plausibly making A both higher-recall AND competitive/faster.
verdict.f2_recommendation: GO (qualified). F-1 shows a real, consistent recall gain (+0.06 nDCG / +0.09 Recall at the value operating point, up to +0.10/+0.14 at small candidate budgets) that is NOT an artifact of quantization and that a persistent index would amplify: F-2 removes F-1's dominant per-call build cost, turning A's latency disadvantage into a likely advantage at matched recall, and lets the operating point sit at small candidate_n where the gain is largest. CAVEAT: this is ONE small in-domain BEIR corpus (SciFact, 5183 docs); the gain should be confirmed on a second, entity-heavier / out-of-domain set (e.g. NFCorpus, FiQA, or a LoTTE slice) before fully funding the 32-512x larger persistent index. The pooled baseline already closes most of the gap at large candidate_n, so F-2's value proposition is specifically 'high recall at low candidate budget / low latency', which must hold on the second corpus too.
